title: Tapa — Pacific bark cloth as paper
subtitle: Beating soaked bark strips until they mat together without a mould
excerpt: Tapa is the traditional Pacific practice of beating the inner bark of the paper mulberry until the fibres mat together into a flexible sheet without any water-suspension on a mould. The same plant material used for Japanese washi is the starting point, but the process is entirely different: there is no vat, no mould, and no couching. Two strips beaten and layered at right angles produce a strong, textile-like sheet. Natural surface variation is part of the finished character.
sourceNotes: Tapa-making tradition documented in Augustin Kramer, The Samoa Islands (1902): public domain. Henry Ling Roth, Studies in Primitive Looms (1918): public domain; Roth documents tapa-beating tools and technique in Pacific tradition. Pacific Island tapa-making is a living tradition with a continuous history predating European contact; the technical description here draws on the public-domain ethnographic record.

glossaryTerms.definition: The Pacific Island term for bark cloth made by beating the inner bark of paper mulberry or related plants. Found across Polynesia, Melanesia, and parts of Micronesia; each island group has its own regional traditions for the decoration and use of the finished cloth.

The process of separating the soft inner bast layer of bark from the rough outer bark. In tapa-making, the inner bast is the material to be beaten; the outer bark is discarded. Soaking overnight makes the separation clean.

The matting together of fibres through physical pressure and moisture, without spinning or weaving. In tapa-making, repeated beating of the wet bast on a flat surface forces the fibres to interlock and mat into a continuous sheet.

content.text: Tapa, the traditional Pacific Island bark cloth, and Japanese washi share the same raw material: the inner bark of the paper mulberry tree (kozo). The two traditions arrive at completely different objects using completely different methods. Washi requires a water vat, a mould, and a suspension technique that distributes the fibres evenly across a mesh.

requires none of this: the fibres are beaten directly on a flat surface, and they mat together by physical force and moisture alone. The result is closer to felt than to paper in its structure: stiffer, more opaque, and with a surface that shows the marks of the beating tool. It is an excellent introduction to non-mould-formed sheet materials.

items.name: Kozo bark strips (dried inner bast), or soaked kozo fibre
items.substitutions: Pre-soaked kozo strips from a papermaking supplier; or strips cut from a kozo fibre bundle

items.name: Smooth rounded stone or glass bottle
items.substitutions: Any heavy, smooth-faced object that can be used as a beater; a glass jar filled with water adds useful weight

items.name: Flat wooden board as beating surface
items.substitutions: A smooth hardwood board at least 30 x 20 cm; the surface must be flat and splinter-free

items.name: Shallow tray of water
items.substitutions: A baking tray or roasting tin with 2-3 cm of water

items.name: Papermaking sponge
items.substitutions: Any soft household sponge for blotting the finished sheet

content.text: Soak the kozo bark strips in cold water overnight. The strips need to absorb water until they are fully pliable. Soaking for at least 12 hours is the minimum; 24 hours is more reliable. The strips will have lightened in colour slightly and will bend without cracking when fully soaked.
Separate the inner bast from the outer bark if the strips have not already been processed.

is straightforward on well-soaked material: peel away the rough outer layer with a fingernail or a blunt blade, exposing the smooth, whitish inner bast. This is the layer you will beat. Keep the inner bast strips wet throughout. If they start to dry, return them to the water tray.

Place one wet strip on the beating board and begin beating with the rounded stone. Use firm, even strokes across the whole width of the strip, working from one end to the other. The strip will spread and thin under the beating, doubling or tripling in width as the fibres are bruised and displaced sideways. Work systematically: beat along the length, then turn the strip 90 degrees and beat across. The sound changes as the fibres mat: a dull thud at the start, moving toward a flatter, less resonant sound as the sheet thickens and the air is expelled.

Wet the strip again as it dries during beating. The fibres need to stay moist to mat correctly; dry fibres break rather than flatten. Keep the water tray beside the board and dip the strip or sponge additional water onto the surface every few minutes.

Beat a second strip to approximately the same thinness. Lay the second strip across the first at right angles (at 90 degrees to the first strip's fibre direction). This cross-layering is the key structural step: a single-layer tapa sheet has directional strength, tearing easily along the fibre direction; a two-layer sheet is strong in both directions, like a woven cloth.
Beat the two layers together. Once the second strip is in place, continue beating the combined stack. The

of the two layers into one happens during this stage: after 5 to 10 minutes of firm beating, the layers become inseparable. The sheet will be denser, stiffer, and more opaque than a single layer. Continue beating until the surface feels even and shows a consistent texture across the whole sheet area.

Transfer the beaten sheet to a smooth drying surface. Lift it carefully from the beating board; it will stick slightly and can tear if rushed. Place it on a smooth board (melamine-faced MDF is ideal) or on a window pane in good light. Smooth any creases with a damp sponge. Place a light flat weight on top to prevent curling while drying. In a warm room, the sheet will be dry in several hours.

Once dry, the sheet can be used as a writing or drawing surface, folded for bookbinding projects, or used as decorative paper. It will be slightly stiffer and thicker than mould-formed paper of the same weight. The natural surface variation (ridges from the beating, visible fibre directions, slight thickness variation) is a quality of the material, not a flaw.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Tapa is forgiving about most things. The three commonest difficulties are under-soaking, dry beating, and not crossing the layers.

items.symptom: The bark strips crack and break during beating instead of spreading
items.cause: The strips were not soaked long enough, or they dried out during the beating session.
items.fix: Return the strips to water and soak for a further few hours. During beating, apply water regularly with a sponge. The material must stay pliable throughout.

items.symptom: The two layers separate when the sheet is dry
items.cause: The layers were not beaten together long enough after the cross-layering step, or they were placed together while both were too dry.
items.fix: Rewet both layers, place them together again, and continue beating for at least 5 more minutes. The felting of the two layers requires sustained wet beating, not just contact.

items.symptom: The finished sheet is very thin and tears easily
items.cause: The individual strips were beaten too thin before cross-layering.
items.fix: Add a third strip to the stack and beat it in. Three layers of moderately beaten bast produce a stronger sheet than two layers that have been beaten very thin.

content.text: Tapa-making shows that a sheet material can be formed without a water suspension or a mould. Once the beating-and-matting sequence is familiar, the same logic applies to amate bark paper from the Mesoamerican tradition. Amate uses fig-bark strips beaten on a flat stone in a similar way. Both traditions also suit natural pigment decoration. Pigment applied to the slightly damp finished sheet takes differently from sized paper.
